pattern: The cost of retargeting every even-indexed value to one residue and every odd-indexed value to another residue splits into two completely independent totals, so trying every pair of distinct residues and adding the two cheapest independent totals finds the optimum.
hints: The even-indexed positions and the odd-indexed positions never influence each other's cost, so work out, for every possible residue, the total cost of retargeting just the even positions to it, and separately the total cost of retargeting just the odd positions to it.

The cost of nudging a single value to a target residue by repeated +1/-1 steps is the shorter of stepping up or stepping down to the nearest number with that residue, which only ever requires comparing the value's own remainder to the target.

With those two small cost tables in hand, just try every pair of two different residues (one for even positions, one for odd) and keep the cheapest combined total — remember the two residues must differ, so the cheapest even residue and the cheapest odd residue cannot always be paired together.

edge_inputs.description: the smallest possible k with a two-element array
a single-element array, so there are no odd positions to satisfy
k at its maximum with the longest array, all minimal values
every value already shares the same residue, forcing the two chosen residues to be distinct anyway
values at the top of the allowed range, testing large-number modulo handling

stress.description: random arrays and moduli across the full allowed scale
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, min(size, 100))
    k = rng.randint(2, 100)
    nums = [rng.randint(1, 10 ** 9) for _ in range(n)]
    return [nums, k]
